one_line_takeaway: MM-MEPA shows that rewriting only the text metadata of image-caption knowledge-base entries (leaving images untouched) is sufficient to poison multimodal RAG retrieval and generation with up to ~92% attack success, and that simple image-metadata cosine-similarity checks and query-paraphrasing defenses both fail to catch it.
key_finding: MM-MEPA is highly effective despite modifying only text metadata. On MMQA, RPois@k exceeds 35% across all four retrievers, and ASR reaches up to 91.6% (SigLIP+BLIP-2: RPois@k=0.865, ACC drops from 0.291 to 0.074, ASR=0.916). On WebQA, RPois@k reaches up to 94.1% (OpenCLIP retrievers) and ASR reaches up to 66.7% (OpenCLIP+BLIP-2). Accuracy degradation from the clean baseline (Table 1) reaches up to ~26.5 percentage points on MMQA (SigLIP+LLaVA: ACC 0.378 -> 0.113) and up to ~33.1 points on WebQA (OpenCLIP+LLaVA: ACC 0.394 -> 0.063). Under the query-paraphrasing defense, attack effectiveness is largely preserved (ASR remains comparable, e.g., SigLIP+BLIP-2 MMQA ASR=0.871 vs 0.916 unparaphrased), indicating the attack is not merely exploiting lexical overlap with the original query phrasing. Under the image-metadata cosine-similarity consistency check, KDE plots (Fig. 2, Fig. 4/Appendix) show poisoned-caption similarity distributions nearly overlapping with clean-caption distributions across all four retrievers on both datasets, and gold-caption cohesion for both datasets stays well above the paper's stated 0.2 detection threshold (Fig. 3) -- meaning simple cosine-similarity thresholding cannot separate poisoned from clean entries. Both tested defenses therefore fail to meaningfully mitigate the attack.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MM-MEPA, a metadata-only poisoning attack on multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (MM-RAG) systems, plus a constrained metadata optimization (CMO) framework that formalizes the attack as maximizing query-metadata embedding alignment subject to a lower-bound constraint on image-metadata cosine similarity (the 'cohesion' constraint). Unlike prior multimodal RAG poisoning attacks (e.g., MM-PoisonRAG, cited as [16]) that perturb both image and caption/metadata, MM-MEPA leaves the image pixels entirely unaltered and only rewrites the associated text metadata (captions/alt-text/tags). Candidate poisoned captions are generated by prompting gpt-4.1-mini (conditioned on image context, target query, and true answer) to produce a plausible-but-incorrect caption; candidates are then filtered by the image-metadata cohesion constraint and the surviving candidate with highest query-alignment is selected (Eqs. 3-8) and injected into the knowledge base.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with no knowledge of the underlying retriever or generator architecture/weights. The attacker can inject new image-metadata pairs or modify metadata of existing KB entries (e.g., by uploading content to platforms, editing public image descriptions/tags, or contributing to open/community-governed datasets), but cannot alter the associated image content. Attacker objective (Eq. 1): maximize the probability that a poisoned entry is ranked in the top-k retrieved results for a target query distribution, thereby hijacking the context passed to the generator to induce a targeted incorrect answer or narrative.
limitations: The paper's own scope limits: only cosine-similarity/CLIP-style embedding retrievers and two open generators (BLIP-2, LLaVA) were tested; no results against retrieval-time defenses beyond paraphrasing and cosine-cohesion checks (e.g., no ensemble/disagreement-based filtering, no fine-grained region-level visual grounding checks) -- the authors explicitly flag these as future work rather than tested mitigations. ASR is defined conservatively as exact-match adoption of the injected caption's stated incorrect answer, which likely undercounts true attack impact from indirect semantic drift (acknowledged by the authors). Candidate caption generation depends on a single commercial LLM (gpt-4.1-mini) as the proposal distribution; transferability of the attack to differently-generated captions is not evaluated. The attack requires a single gold image per query with reliable image-caption supervision (curated MMQA/WebQA subsets), so generalization to noisier, larger-scale real-world KBs is not directly demonstrated.

datasets_used: MultimodalQA (MMQA) -- 230 curated queries, k=1 retrieval, single gold image-caption pair per question
WebQA -- 2,511 curated queries, k=2 retrieval, multiple valid image-caption pairs per question, canonical exact-match (EM) answer field
quantitative_results: MMQA clean baseline (Table 1): ROrig@k ranges 0.152 (SigLIP) to 0.978 (OpenCLIP); ACC ranges 0.291 (SigLIP+BLIP-2) to 0.596 (CLIP+LLaVA)
WebQA clean baseline (Table 1): ROrig@k ranges 0.157 (SigLIP) to 0.785 (OpenCLIP); ACC ranges 0.324 (SigLIP+BLIP-2) to 0.394 (OpenCLIP+LLaVA)
MMQA poisoned (Table 2): RPois@k 0.383-0.865; ACC drops of 0.117-0.265; ASR 0.366-0.916 (max ASR: SigLIP+BLIP-2 = 0.916)
WebQA poisoned (Table 2): RPois@k 0.114-0.941 (max: OpenCLIP = 0.941); ACC drops of 0.043-0.331; ASR 0.242-0.667 (max ASR: OpenCLIP+BLIP-2 = 0.667)
MMQA paraphrased-query robustness (Table 3): ASR remains comparable, e.g. SigLIP+BLIP-2 ASR=0.871 (vs 0.916 unparaphrased)
WebQA paraphrased-query robustness (Table 3): ASR remains comparable, e.g. OpenCLIP+BLIP-2 ASR=0.629 (vs 0.667 unparaphrased)

Image-text cohesion detection threshold stated at 0.2 cosine similarity (Fig. 3); gold-caption cohesion stays well above this threshold across retrievers/datasets, and poisoned-caption similarity distributions closely overlap clean-caption distributions (Fig. 2, Appendix Fig. 4), showing the threshold-based defense cannot distinguish poisoned from clean entries
baselines_compared: Clean (unpoisoned) MM-RAG performance across the same 4 retrievers x 2 generators (Table 1) used as the reference point for measuring degradation

MM-PoisonRAG [16] -- prior multimodal poisoning attack that perturbs both image and text; MM-MEPA follows its evaluation protocol/curated query subsets but differs by perturbing metadata only, leaving images unaltered
Query-paraphrasing defense [22], adapted from LLM jailbreak defense literature, tested as a mitigation
Image-metadata cosine-similarity consistency check tested as a mitigation, with a stated 0.2 similarity detection threshold
